texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Influence of capillary condensation effects on mass transport through porous membranes
texts.text: Influence of capillary condensation effects on mass transport through porous membranes

texts.label: text

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The importance of capillary condensation effects in connection with properly quantifying the rate of mass transport through porous media has been already described (J. Coll. Interf. Sci. 110 (1986) 544; J. Membr. Sci. 66 (1992) 259; J. Membr. Sci. 182 (2001) 91). Howe v er, there is still not enough work done in order to understand, quantify and possibly exploit this phenomenon. In this work permeation and binary diffusion experiments ha v e been performed at ambient temperature in combination with measurements of the adsorption isotherms using porous Vycor glass possessing a mean pore radius of approximately 4 nm. Main focus was set on a comparison of the transport of (a) permanent gases (helium, nitrogen, methane and hydrogen) and (b) condensable gases (butane, propane and freon 112) as a function of the pressure conditions in the membrane. The adsorption isotherms of the condensable gases were measured using a v olumetric method. The results indicate the occurrence of capillary condensation at a reduced pressure P / P 0 of approximately 0.8 for all three gases. There is a good agreement between the v olume of condensate at saturation and the pore v olume determined in independent porosimetric studies. Results of a large set of experiments with butane re v ealed that the permeation is only a function of the mean gas pressure in the membrane. This contradicts the a v ailable models suggested in (J. Coll. Interf. Sci. 110 (1986) 544; J. Membr. Sci. 66 (1992) 259) predicting that the transport mechanism in pores changes with different pressure conditions. Finally, in binary isobaric diffusion experiments with pure helium on one side of the membrane and pure butane on the other, a remarkable increase in selecti v ity of the transport process with increasing pressure in the membrane was obser v ed. # 2003 Else v ier B.V. All rights reserved.

Sci. 110 (1986) 544; J. Membr. Sci. 66 (1992) 259; J. Membr. Sci. 182 (2001) 91). Howe v er, there is still not enough work done in order to understand, quantify and possibly exploit this phenomenon. In this work permeation and binary diffusion experiments ha v e been performed at ambient temperature in combination with measurements of the adsorption isotherms using porous Vycor glass possessing a mean pore radius of approximately 4 nm. Main focus was set on a comparison of the transport of (a) permanent gases (helium, nitrogen, methane and hydrogen) and (b) condensable gases (butane, propane and freon 112) as a function of the pressure conditions in the membrane. The adsorption isotherms of the condensable gases were measured using a v olumetric method. The results indicate the occurrence of capillary condensation at a reduced pressure P / P 0 of approximately 0.8 for all three gases. There is a good agreement between the v olume of condensate at saturation and the pore v olume determined in independent porosimetric studies. Results of a large set of experiments with butane re v ealed that the permeation is only a function of the mean gas pressure in the membrane. This contradicts the a v ailable models suggested in (J. Coll. Interf. Sci. 110 (1986) 544; J. Membr. Sci. 66 (1992) 259) predicting that the transport mechanism in pores changes with different pressure conditions. Finally, in binary isobaric diffusion experiments with pure helium on one side of the membrane and pure butane on the other, a remarkable increase in selecti v ity of the transport process with increasing pressure in the membrane was obser v ed. # 2003 Else v ier B.V. All rights reserved.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The interest in membrane processes to separate gas mixtures using inorganic membranes has increased considerably [4  /11]. The ad v antage of inorganic membranes in comparison with polymeric membranes is essentially their thermal and chemical stability. Moreo v er, the gas fluxes through inorganic membranes are usually higher. There are different mechanisms of the gas transport in polymeric and inorganic membranes. The solution diffusion model is widely used for the description of transport in polymeric membranes [12]. The transport is di v ided into three consecuti v e steps * /sorption in membrane, diffusion through membrane and desorption on the other side of membrane. The slow diffusion of the permeating gas is typically the rate determinating step. The mass transport through pores of inorganic membranes is more complex.
texts.text: The interest in membrane processes to separate gas mixtures using inorganic membranes has increased considerably [4  /11]. The ad v antage of inorganic membranes in comparison with polymeric membranes is essentially their thermal and chemical stability. Moreo v er, the gas fluxes through inorganic membranes are usually higher. There are different mechanisms of the gas transport in polymeric and inorganic membranes. The solution diffusion model is widely used for the description of transport in polymeric membranes [12]. The transport is di v ided into three consecuti v e steps * /sorption in membrane, diffusion through membrane and desorption on the other side of membrane. The slow diffusion of the permeating gas is typically the rate determinating step. The mass transport through pores of inorganic membranes is more complex.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the case of gas flow through inorganic membranes with v ery small pores (size of about se v eral nanometres), Knudsen diffusion is usually the dominant mechanism of the transport. The separation factor for this case is proportional to the ratio of the reciprocal square roots of molecular weights of the substances (e.g. [13]). It is also well known that the transport through small pores is influenced by the interactions of permeating molecules with the pore walls. These interactions could lead to their sorption on the pore surface. The sorbed layer of molecules might not be immobile and the transport of sorbed molecules can occur from one site to another in the direction of decreasing loading. Adsorbable gases ha v e higher permeabilities in porous material than predicted from the equations for Knudsen and Poisseuille flows. This additional contribution to the transport is called surface diffusion [14  / 17].
texts.text: In the case of gas flow through inorganic membranes with v ery small pores (size of about se v eral nanometres), Knudsen diffusion is usually the dominant mechanism of the transport. The separation factor for this case is proportional to the ratio of the reciprocal square roots of molecular weights of the substances (e.g. [13]). It is also well known that the transport through small pores is influenced by the interactions of permeating molecules with the pore walls. These interactions could lead to their sorption on the pore surface. The sorbed layer of molecules might not be immobile and the transport of sorbed molecules can occur from one site to another in the direction of decreasing loading. Adsorbable gases ha v e higher permeabilities in porous material than predicted from the equations for Knudsen and Poisseuille flows. This additional contribution to the transport is called surface diffusion [14  / 17].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Adsorbate  /adsorbate interactions are responsible for the possible formation of multilayers. The multilayer adsorption usually already starts before the formation of the monolayer is finished. Gas condensation in small pores can occur under pressure conditions close to the partial pressure of the condensable gas. Capillary condensation typically begins before the completion of multilayer adsorption. Howe v er this mechanism appears to be least understood. All these mentioned phenomena can remarkably influence the efficiency of gas separation processes. More details concerning surface diffusion, multilayer surface diffusion and capillary condensate flow were recently published by Choi et al. [18,19].
texts.text: Adsorbate  /adsorbate interactions are responsible for the possible formation of multilayers. The multilayer adsorption usually already starts before the formation of the monolayer is finished. Gas condensation in small pores can occur under pressure conditions close to the partial pressure of the condensable gas. Capillary condensation typically begins before the completion of multilayer adsorption. Howe v er this mechanism appears to be least understood. All these mentioned phenomena can remarkably influence the efficiency of gas separation processes. More details concerning surface diffusion, multilayer surface diffusion and capillary condensate flow were recently published by Choi et al. [18,19].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is the purpose of this paper to supply additional experimental data as a basis to quantify the mass transport of condensable gases.
texts.text: It is the purpose of this paper to supply additional experimental data as a basis to quantify the mass transport of condensable gases.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Theory
texts.text: 2. Theory

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The gas transport through small pores under condensation conditions has not been described satisfactory yet. There are se v eral papers dealing with capillary condensation in small pores [1  / 4,16  /18]. Howe v er, there is still no agreement how to predict the flux of permeating substances if capillary condensation occurs in the pores of membrane. The six different flow modes proposed by Lee and Hwang [1] or by Uhlhorn et al. [2] are widely used for the description of the transport of condensable gas through small pores (Fig. 1). In case C1, there is no condensation in pores and the total flux is the sum of Knudsen and surface diffusion contributions. In the other cases depicted in Fig. 1 condensation occurs in the pores. Depending on the pressure conditions, this happens in parts of the pores (cases C2 and C4) or in the whole pores (cases C3, C5 and C6). The cases C3, C5 and C6 are distinguished by the presence of the meniscus and its position in the pores, which is gi v en by the relation between the thickness of adsorbed layer t and pore radius r .
texts.text: The gas transport through small pores under condensation conditions has not been described satisfactory yet. There are se v eral papers dealing with capillary condensation in small pores [1  / 4,16  /18]. Howe v er, there is still no agreement how to predict the flux of permeating substances if capillary condensation occurs in the pores of membrane. The six different flow modes proposed by Lee and Hwang [1] or by Uhlhorn et al. [2] are widely used for the description of the transport of condensable gas through small pores (Fig. 1). In case C1, there is no condensation in pores and the total flux is the sum of Knudsen and surface diffusion contributions. In the other cases depicted in Fig. 1 condensation occurs in the pores. Depending on the pressure conditions, this happens in parts of the pores (cases C2 and C4) or in the whole pores (cases C3, C5 and C6). The cases C3, C5 and C6 are distinguished by the presence of the meniscus and its position in the pores, which is gi v en by the relation between the thickness of adsorbed layer t and pore radius r .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is difficult to v erify the proposed mass transport model since the inorganic porous membranes do not ha v e uniform pore sizes. Further, the pores are not of cylindrical shape as assumed in [1,2]. Therefore it is not straightforward to distinguish between the contributions of the indi v idual mechanisms described. The ratio of the indi v idual contributions of different transport mechanisms will change depending on the pore radius. Also the pressure conditions for capillary condensation can be quite di v ers. Usually the condition where capillary condensation occurs is described by the Kel v in equation:

Further, the pores are not of cylindrical shape as assumed in [1,2]. Therefore it is not straightforward to distinguish between the contributions of the indi v idual mechanisms described. The ratio of the indi v idual contributions of different transport mechanisms will change depending on the pore radius. Also the pressure conditions for capillary condensation can be quite di v ers. Usually the condition where capillary condensation occurs is described by the Kel v in equation:

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Porous structure of applied membrane
texts.text: 3.1. Porous structure of applied membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A Vycor glass tube (outer radius 5.2 mm, inner radius 4 mm, length 29.3 mm) was used for the experiments. The porous structure of Vycor was determined by nitrogen adsorption (ASAP 20100, Micromeritics, USA) and helium pycnometry (AutoPycnometer 1320, Micromeritics, USA). Mercury porosimetry was not applicable since the pores were found to be too small for this method.
texts.text: A Vycor glass tube (outer radius 5.2 mm, inner radius 4 mm, length 29.3 mm) was used for the experiments. The porous structure of Vycor was determined by nitrogen adsorption (ASAP 20100, Micromeritics, USA) and helium pycnometry (AutoPycnometer 1320, Micromeritics, USA). Mercury porosimetry was not applicable since the pores were found to be too small for this method.

texts.text: bubble flow meter. The use of a v acuum pump allowed to realize measurements under subatmospheric pressure conditions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The experiments with butane and helium were performed using an isobaric diffusion experiment (Fig. 3b). The pure gases were fed under steady state conditions at both inlets into the membrane cell. Val v es were located at the outlets to set the same pressure on both sides of the membrane ( P 1 / P 2 / P ) and to a v oid a v iscous flux dri v en by an o v erall pressure gradient. Thus the gases diffused through the membrane only due to a concentration difference. Bubble flow meters were situated at both outlets to measure the flow rates of the mixtures. To analyse the gas composition a thermal conducti v ity detector was used at one outlet.
texts.text: The experiments with butane and helium were performed using an isobaric diffusion experiment (Fig. 3b). The pure gases were fed under steady state conditions at both inlets into the membrane cell. Val v es were located at the outlets to set the same pressure on both sides of the membrane ( P 1 / P 2 / P ) and to a v oid a v iscous flux dri v en by an o v erall pressure gradient. Thus the gases diffused through the membrane only due to a concentration difference. Bubble flow meters were situated at both outlets to measure the flow rates of the mixtures. To analyse the gas composition a thermal conducti v ity detector was used at one outlet.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3. Adsorption measurements
texts.text: 3.3. Adsorption measurements

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The adsorption isotherms of three condensable gases (butane, propane and freon 112) were measured using a v olumetric method (Fig. 4). The measurement was carried out as follows: after e v acuation of the whole apparatus, the v olume V 2 (or also V 3 which was used for higher pressures) was filled with adsorbable gas at the required pressure. Then the v al v e connecting the v olume V 2 (or V 2 and V 3) with the cell containing the Vycor sample ( v olume V 1 ) was opened. After reaching equilibrium (approximately after 10 min) the pressure was measured. From the mass balance the adsorbed amount of gas was calculated. Nonideal beha v iour of gases was taken into account by considering the compressibility factor.
texts.text: The adsorption isotherms of three condensable gases (butane, propane and freon 112) were measured using a v olumetric method (Fig. 4). The measurement was carried out as follows: after e v acuation of the whole apparatus, the v olume V 2 (or also V 3 which was used for higher pressures) was filled with adsorbable gas at the required pressure. Then the v al v e connecting the v olume V 2 (or V 2 and V 3) with the cell containing the Vycor sample ( v olume V 1 ) was opened. After reaching equilibrium (approximately after 10 min) the pressure was measured. From the mass balance the adsorbed amount of gas was calculated. Nonideal beha v iour of gases was taken into account by considering the compressibility factor.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Results and discussion
texts.text: 4. Results and discussion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The obtained permeation plots for nonadsorbable gases (hydrogen, helium, nitrogen and methane) and adsorbable gases (butane, propane and freon 112) are shown in Fig. 5. It can be obser v ed that the permeability of the nonadsorbable gases is independent on the mean gas pressure in the membrane. This is in complete agreement with the expected Knudsen flux in the pores. In the case of adsorbable gases, the permeability increases with increasing mean gas pressure in the membrane. The flux of butane rises from 3 /10 11 mol/s Pa at 1 bar to 1.7 /10 10 mol/s Pa at 1.8 bar. This confirms that a different type of transport mechanism contributes to the flux of adsorbable gas through the pores.
texts.text: The obtained permeation plots for nonadsorbable gases (hydrogen, helium, nitrogen and methane) and adsorbable gases (butane, propane and freon 112) are shown in Fig. 5. It can be obser v ed that the permeability of the nonadsorbable gases is independent on the mean gas pressure in the membrane. This is in complete agreement with the expected Knudsen flux in the pores. In the case of adsorbable gases, the permeability increases with increasing mean gas pressure in the membrane. The flux of butane rises from 3 /10 11 mol/s Pa at 1 bar to 1.7 /10 10 mol/s Pa at 1.8 bar. This confirms that a different type of transport mechanism contributes to the flux of adsorbable gas through the pores.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to e v aluate the transport mechanisms the adsorption isotherms of the condensable gases were measured at ambient temperature. The adsorption isotherms are shown in Fig. 6. The course of the isotherms is similar and it indicates the occurrence of capillary condensation at a reduced pressure P / P 0 of approximately 0.78 for butane, 0.83 for freon 112 and 0.89 for propane. There is good agreement between the v olume of condensate V corresponding to the maximum of adsorbed amount N max and the pore v olume determined

texts.text: from independent porosimetric studies, see Table 1. The adsorbed (condensate) amount of butane N max is approximately 1.64 /10 3 mol/g Vycor, 1.64 /10 3 mol of the liquid butane corresponds to 0.164 cm 3 of liquid/g, ( V / N max M / r ; M Bu /58 g/mol, r Bu liq /0.58 g/cm 3 at 23 8 C).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To study the transport mechanisms of adsorbable gases, a large number of permeation experiments with butane was carried out. From the results depicted in Fig. 5 it can be seen that there is a large difference between the permeability of butane for mean pressures below and abo v e 1 bar. The dependence of butane permeability on mean pressure for low v alues is linear in the mean pressure range of 0  /0.9 bar with the intercept which is related to the Knudsen diffusi v ity. The result is in agreement with the permeability of nonadsorbable gases. But the slope is not zero e v en for v ery small v alues of the mean pressure. This means that there is also another contribution to the butane flux under these conditions, perhaps multilayer diffusion. For mean pressures higher than 1 bar, the permeability of butane increases steeply.
texts.text: To study the transport mechanisms of adsorbable gases, a large number of permeation experiments with butane was carried out. From the results depicted in Fig. 5 it can be seen that there is a large difference between the permeability of butane for mean pressures below and abo v e 1 bar. The dependence of butane permeability on mean pressure for low v alues is linear in the mean pressure range of 0  /0.9 bar with the intercept which is related to the Knudsen diffusi v ity. The result is in agreement with the permeability of nonadsorbable gases. But the slope is not zero e v en for v ery small v alues of the mean pressure. This means that there is also another contribution to the butane flux under these conditions, perhaps multilayer diffusion. For mean pressures higher than 1 bar, the permeability of butane increases steeply.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Subsequently a series of experiments was performed in order to find some e v idence for butane condensation in pores of Vycor membrane. In Fig. 7 the obser v ed dependence of butane permeability on mean pressure is shown for different feed pressures P 1 . According to the transport model proposed in [1,2] the flow modes change for different pressure conditions. This model predicts that different permeabilities of butane would be obtained for the same mean pressure ( P 1  / P 2 )/2 but different v alues of P 1 and P 2. According this model the presence of condensation is indicated by steeply decreasing the permeability. For example if condensation occurs at 1.74 bar (according to Kel v in equation for butane at pore radii lower than 4 nm, at 23 8 C) the permeability for P 1 /1.6 and P 2 /1.4 bar (no condensation) will be different than for P 1 /2 and P 2 /1 (condensation conditions) e v en if the mean pressures in both cases are the same. Howe v er, as a result of our experimental in v estigation the contrary was found to be true. Also the v alues of permeability for other pressure conditions with the same mean pressure (e.g. P 1 /1.8 and P 2 / 1.2; P 1 /1.9 and P 2 /1.1) are approximately equal (Fig. 7).
texts.text: Subsequently a series of experiments was performed in order to find some e v idence for butane condensation in pores of Vycor membrane. In Fig. 7 the obser v ed dependence of butane permeability on mean pressure is shown for different feed pressures P 1 . According to the transport model proposed in [1,2] the flow modes change for different pressure conditions. This model predicts that different permeabilities of butane would be obtained for the same mean pressure ( P 1  / P 2 )/2 but different v alues of P 1 and P 2. According this model the presence of condensation is indicated by steeply decreasing the permeability. For example if condensation occurs at 1.74 bar (according to Kel v in equation for butane at pore radii lower than 4 nm, at 23 8 C) the permeability for P 1 /1.6 and P 2 /1.4 bar (no condensation) will be different than for P 1 /2 and P 2 /1 (condensation conditions) e v en if the mean pressures in both cases are the same. Howe v er, as a result of our experimental in v estigation the contrary was found to be true. Also the v alues of permeability for other pressure conditions with the same mean pressure (e.g. P 1 /1.8 and P 2 / 1.2; P 1 /1.9 and P 2 /1.1) are approximately equal (Fig. 7).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All experimental points shown in Fig. 7 (different pressure conditions) were analysed by applying Kel v in equation. The result di v ides the experimental data in two groups. The empty points reflect experiments where condensation in pores should not occur ( P 1 B / P con /1.74 bar). Full points represent the situation with a feed pressure P 1 higher than the calculated condensation pressure P con ( P 2 B / P con ). From the comparison of

texts.orig: empty and full points it could be seen that there is no difference in the experimentally obtained permeabilities of both cases.
texts.text: empty and full points it could be seen that there is no difference in the experimentally obtained permeabilities of both cases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: By nitrogen adsorption (Fig. 2) small pores with radii about 1 nm were detected in Vycor glass. In these small pores the condensation of butane starts below 1 bar (Table 2). Moreo v er, the adsorption of butane on pore surface diminishes the free pore size and therefore the condensation of butane starts e v en at lower pressure than predicted by Kel v in equation. Increasing the butane pressure abo v e 1.74 bar, the condensation occurs in the existing pores with r  /4 nm. The strong decrease in butane permeability, in case of condensation predicted by the models suggested in [1,2] was not obser v ed in our experiments. It appears to be possible that droplets of liquids created during the condensation are expelled from the pores by pressure gradient of butane in the gas phase and thus the liquid does not close the pores.
texts.text: By nitrogen adsorption (Fig. 2) small pores with radii about 1 nm were detected in Vycor glass. In these small pores the condensation of butane starts below 1 bar (Table 2). Moreo v er, the adsorption of butane on pore surface diminishes the free pore size and therefore the condensation of butane starts e v en at lower pressure than predicted by Kel v in equation. Increasing the butane pressure abo v e 1.74 bar, the condensation occurs in the existing pores with r  /4 nm. The strong decrease in butane permeability, in case of condensation predicted by the models suggested in [1,2] was not obser v ed in our experiments. It appears to be possible that droplets of liquids created during the condensation are expelled from the pores by pressure gradient of butane in the gas phase and thus the liquid does not close the pores.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Similar results of the permeation experiment as for butane were obtained for the other condensable gases propane and freon 112. Howe v er their permeation was not studied for different pressure conditions in the same detail.
texts.text: Similar results of the permeation experiment as for butane were obtained for the other condensable gases propane and freon 112. Howe v er their permeation was not studied for different pressure conditions in the same detail.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: membrane. The gases diffuse through the membrane because of the existing concentration differences. The obtained results are depicted in Fig. 8. It can be seen that the butane flux is larger than the flux of helium in the whole pressure range. Further, the butane flux increases with increasing o v erall pressure but this flux increase is less than that obtained in the permeation experiment (compare Fig. 5). Contrary to the butane flux the mass transfer of helium decreases with increasing pressure. This could be attributed to the lowering of pore radius by the adsorbed and condensed butane. In Fig. 8 the ratio of the butane to helium flux v ersus the o v erall pressure is also illustrated. The results are an example that the flux ratio can be changed significantly reaching the condensation range of one of the components of a mixture.
texts.text: membrane. The gases diffuse through the membrane because of the existing concentration differences. The obtained results are depicted in Fig. 8. Further, the butane flux increases with increasing o v erall pressure but this flux increase is less than that obtained in the permeation experiment (compare Fig. 5). Contrary to the butane flux the mass transfer of helium decreases with increasing pressure. This could be attributed to the lowering of pore radius by the adsorbed and condensed butane. In Fig. 8 the ratio of the butane to helium flux v ersus the o v erall pressure is also illustrated. The results are an example that the flux ratio can be changed significantly reaching the condensation range of one of the components of a mixture.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusion
texts.text: 5. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Permeation of permanent and condensable gases through Vycor glass was studied experimentally. In the case of pure permanent gases only Knudsen diffusion was obser v ed. For condensable gases another contribution becomes rele v ant.
texts.text: Permeation of permanent and condensable gases through Vycor glass was studied experimentally. In the case of pure permanent gases only Knudsen diffusion was obser v ed. For condensable gases another contribution becomes rele v ant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the gases studied in this work the capillary condensation in pores was detected by adsorption measurements for P / P 0 around 0.8. Analysing the influence of capillary condensation in pores on the mass transport appears to be difficult. A main reason is that the inorganic porous membranes do not ha v e a uniform pore size and the pores are not of cylindrical shape. E v en for v ery narrow pore size distribution and cylindrical pores the pressure conditions where capillary condensation occurs change significantly.
texts.text: For the gases studied in this work the capillary condensation in pores was detected by adsorption measurements for P / P 0 around 0.8. Analysing the influence of capillary condensation in pores on the mass transport appears to be difficult. A main reason is that the inorganic porous membranes do not ha v e a uniform pore size and the pores are not of cylindrical shape. E v en for v ery narrow pore size distribution and cylindrical pores the pressure conditions where capillary condensation occurs change significantly.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In contrast to other studies [1,2] in our work an increase of permeability with increasing mean pressure in pores was obser v ed. The flux increase of condensable gas with increasing pressure was larger in the single gas permeation experiment than in the isobaric diffusion experiment with a condensable and permanent gas. In the latter case a flux decrease of the permanent gas with increasing pressure was obser v ed.
texts.text: In contrast to other studies [1,2] in our work an increase of permeability with increasing mean pressure in pores was obser v ed. The flux increase of condensable gas with increasing pressure was larger in the single gas permeation experiment than in the isobaric diffusion experiment with a condensable and permanent gas. In the latter case a flux decrease of the permanent gas with increasing pressure was obser v ed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A proper explanation of the increase in butane permeation with increasing mean pressure in pores is not possible solely on the basis of the mentioned experimental results. There is no clear e v idence about the existence of liquid butane in the pores during the permeation experiments performed. To better understand the transport in pores under conditions of capillary condensation it appears to be necessary to conduct additional transport experiments with liquid butane. Such experiments are currently underway.
texts.text: A proper explanation of the increase in butane permeation with increasing mean pressure in pores is not possible solely on the basis of the mentioned experimental results. There is no clear e v idence about the existence of liquid butane in the pores during the permeation experiments performed. To better understand the transport in pores under conditions of capillary condensation it appears to be necessary to conduct additional transport experiments with liquid butane. Such experiments are currently underway.
